{{Infobox ancient site |name=Kinegamori Kofun |image=[[File:Kinegamori-kofun zenkei.JPG|280px]]
Complete view of the burial mound (front section in foreground, rear section in back right) |group={{ill|Unai Aotsu Kofun Group|ja|宇内青津古墳群}} |location={{ill|Inarizuka, Aizubange|ja|稲荷塚}}, {{ill|Aizubange|ja|会津坂下町}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}} |coordinates={{Coord|37|33|58.73|N|139|48|34.89|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kinegamori Kofun}} |type={{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |size=45.6 m mound length
2 m height |constructed=Late 3rd to early 4th century |burial=Unknown |buried= |artifacts={{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} |cultural_property=Fukushima Prefecture Designated Historic Site: "Kinegamori Kofun" |notes=Possibly the oldest kofun in the Tohoku region }} '''Kinegamori Kofun''' ({{ill|Kinegamori Kofun|ja|杵ガ森古墳}}) is a {{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} located in {{ill|Aizubange|ja|会津坂下町}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}. It is part of the {{ill|Unai Aotsu Kofun Group|ja|宇内青津古墳群}} and is designated as a Fukushima Prefecture Historic Site. Kinegamori Kofun is recognized as one of the oldest kofun in the Tohoku region. == Overview == Located west of the city center of Aizubange, along the western bank of the former Miyagawa River (Tsurunumagawa), this keyhole-shaped burial mound faces south. Surrounding it are other ancient burial mounds, including Usagamori Kofun and Maigamori Kofun (now disappeared), which illustrate the region's prosperity during the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. The rear section of Kinegamori Kofun is a two-tiered construction, while the details of the front section are unclear due to erosion. Its distinctive paddle-shaped design resembles that of the earliest keyhole-shaped mound, {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳}}, in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, though Kinegamori is about one-sixth the size of Hashihaka. The mound is encircled by a moat. The mound was first noted in the historical text ''Shinpen Aizu Fudoki'' as a mound, but it was officially identified during an archaeological survey in 1961. Before excavations, only part of the rear section was visible, with the remainder buried under rice fields. Excavations during 1990 revealed that the mound, previously thought to be circular, was actually a keyhole-shaped kofun. Additional findings included a surrounding moat, pit dwellings, and 10 {{ill|Square burial mounds|ja|方形周溝墓}}. Artifacts such as {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} suggest the mound was constructed in the late 3rd to early 4th century, making it one of the oldest kofun in Tohoku. == Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows: * Total length: 45.6 meters * Rear section: * Diameter: 25.6 meters * Height: ~2 meters * Front section: * Length: 24 meters * Width: 19 meters == Inarizuka Site == [[File:Inarizuka-iseki (Aizubange).JPG|thumb|200px|right|Inarizuka Site]] The '''Inarizuka Site''' ({{ill|Inarizuka Site|ja|稲荷塚遺跡}}) is an early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} site near Kinegamori Kofun. Excavations from 1990 to 1991 uncovered 15 pit dwellings, 3 {{ill|pillar-supported buildings|ja|掘立柱建物}}, and 10 square burial mounds. These findings suggest that the site initially functioned as a settlement during the early Kofun period. Kinegamori Kofun was later constructed on the site, with additional burial mounds built nearby for individuals associated with the kofun. In 2017, an additional 13 square burial mounds were discovered, bringing the total to 23, making it the largest collection of square burial mounds in Fukushima Prefecture[http://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20171122-222147.php "Fukushima Prefecture’s Largest 'Square Burial Mound Group' Discovered at Inarizuka Site"] (''Minyu'', November 22, 2017).. == Cultural Property == === Fukushima Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties === * Historic Site * Kinegamori Kofun - Designated on March 22, 1996[http://www.town.aizubange.fukushima.jp/soshiki/30/285.html "List of Cultural Properties in Aizubange"] (Aizubange Town website).. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == {{small|References used in this article}} * Historic Site Description Panel * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1993|chapter=Kinegamori Kofun|title={{ill|Historical Place names of Japan|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 7: Fukushima Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490077|ref={{Harvid|Kinegamori Kofun (Heibonsha)|1993}}}} * Hidehito Tsuji, *The Foundation of Tohoku Kofun Research: Aizu Otsukayama Kofun*, "Learning from Historical Sites" Series 029, Shinseisha, 2006. == Further Reading == {{small|Sources not directly used in the article}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1991|chapter=|title=Excavation Report on Kinegamori Kofun and Inarizuka Site (Aizubange Town Cultural Property Report No. 22)|publisher=Aizubange Town Board of Education|isbn=|ref=}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1995|chapter=|title=Preservation Report of Kinegamori Kofun (Aizubange Town Cultural Property Report No. 33)|publisher=Aizubange Town Board of Education|isbn=|ref=}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Kinegamori-kofun}} * [http://www.town.aizubange.fukushima.jp/soshiki/30/282.html Kinegamori Kofun] - {{Infobox ancient site |name=Kinegamori Kofun |image=[[File:Kinegamori-kofun zenkei.JPG|280px]]
Complete view of the burial mound (front section in foreground, rear section in back right) |group={{ill|Unai Aotsu Kofun Group|ja|宇内青津古墳群}} |location={{ill|Inarizuka, Aizubange|ja|稲荷塚}}, {{ill|Aizubange|ja|会津坂下町}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}} |coordinates={{Coord|37|33|58.73|N|139|48|34.89|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kinegamori Kofun}} |type={{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |size=45.6 m mound length
2 m height |constructed=Late 3rd to early 4th century |burial=Unknown |buried= |artifacts={{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} |cultural_property=Fukushima Prefecture Designated Historic Site: "Kinegamori Kofun" |notes=Possibly the oldest kofun in the Tohoku region }} '''Kinegamori Kofun''' ({{ill|Kinegamori Kofun|ja|杵ガ森古墳}}) is a {{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} located in {{ill|Aizubange|ja|会津坂下町}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}. It is part of the {{ill|Unai Aotsu Kofun Group|ja|宇内青津古墳群}} and is designated as a Fukushima Prefecture Historic Site. Kinegamori Kofun is recognized as one of the oldest kofun in the Tohoku region. == Overview == Located west of the city center of Aizubange, along the western bank of the former Miyagawa River (Tsurunumagawa), this keyhole-shaped burial mound faces south. Surrounding it are other ancient burial mounds, including Usagamori Kofun and Maigamori Kofun (now disappeared), which illustrate the region's prosperity during the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. The rear section of Kinegamori Kofun is a two-tiered construction, while the details of the front section are unclear due to erosion. Its distinctive paddle-shaped design resembles that of the earliest keyhole-shaped mound, {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳}}, in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, though Kinegamori is about one-sixth the size of Hashihaka. The mound is encircled by a moat. The mound was first noted in the historical text ''Shinpen Aizu Fudoki'' as a mound, but it was officially identified during an archaeological survey in 1961. Before excavations, only part of the rear section was visible, with the remainder buried under rice fields. Excavations during 1990 revealed that the mound, previously thought to be circular, was actually a keyhole-shaped kofun. Additional findings included a surrounding moat, pit dwellings, and 10 {{ill|Square burial mounds|ja|方形周溝墓}}. Artifacts such as {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} suggest the mound was constructed in the late 3rd to early 4th century, making it one of the oldest kofun in Tohoku. == Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows: * Total length: 45.6 meters * Rear section: * Diameter: 25.6 meters * Height: ~2 meters * Front section: * Length: 24 meters * Width: 19 meters == Inarizuka Site == [[File:Inarizuka-iseki (Aizubange).JPG|thumb|200px|right|Inarizuka Site]] The '''Inarizuka Site''' ({{ill|Inarizuka Site|ja|稲荷塚遺跡}}) is an early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} site near Kinegamori Kofun. Excavations from 1990 to 1991 uncovered 15 pit dwellings, 3 {{ill|pillar-supported buildings|ja|掘立柱建物}}, and 10 square burial mounds. These findings suggest that the site initially functioned as a settlement during the early Kofun period. Kinegamori Kofun was later constructed on the site, with additional burial mounds built nearby for individuals associated with the kofun. In 2017, an additional 13 square burial mounds were discovered, bringing the total to 23, making it the largest collection of square burial mounds in Fukushima Prefecture[http://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20171122-222147.php "Fukushima Prefecture’s Largest 'Square Burial Mound Group' Discovered at Inarizuka Site"] (''Minyu'', November 22, 2017).. == Cultural Property == === Fukushima Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties === * Historic Site * Kinegamori Kofun - Designated on March 22, 1996[http://www.town.aizubange.fukushima.jp/soshiki/30/285.html "List of Cultural Properties in Aizubange"] (Aizubange Town website).. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == {{small|References used in this article}} * Historic Site Description Panel * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1993|chapter=Kinegamori Kofun|title={{ill|Historical Place names of Japan|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 7: Fukushima Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490077|ref={{Harvid|Kinegamori Kofun (Heibonsha)|1993}}}} * Hidehito Tsuji, *The Foundation of Tohoku Kofun Research: Aizu Otsukayama Kofun*, "Learning from Historical Sites" Series 029, Shinseisha, 2006. == Further Reading == {{small|Sources not directly used in the article}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1991|chapter=|title=Excavation Report on Kinegamori Kofun and Inarizuka Site (Aizubange Town Cultural Property Report No. 22)|publisher=Aizubange Town Board of Education|isbn=|ref=}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1995|chapter=|title=Preservation Report of Kinegamori Kofun (Aizubange Town Cultural Property Report No. 33)|publisher=Aizubange Town Board of Education|isbn=|ref=}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Kinegamori-kofun}} * [http://www.town.aizubange.fukushima.jp/soshiki/30/282.html Kinegamori Kofun] - {{Infobox ancient site |name=Kinegamori Kofun |image=[[File:Kinegamori-kofun zenkei.JPG|280px]]
Complete view of the burial mound (front section in foreground, rear section in back right) |group={{ill|Unai Aotsu Kofun Group|ja|宇内青津古墳群}} |location={{ill|Inarizuka, Aizubange|ja|稲荷塚}}, {{ill|Aizubange|ja|会津坂下町}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}} |coordinates={{Coord|37|33|58.73|N|139|48|34.89|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kinegamori Kofun}} |type={{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |size=45.6 m mound length
2 m height |constructed=Late 3rd to early 4th century |burial=Unknown |buried= |artifacts={{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} |cultural_property=Fukushima Prefecture Designated Historic Site: "Kinegamori Kofun" |notes=Possibly the oldest kofun in the Tohoku region }} '''Kinegamori Kofun''' ({{ill|Kinegamori Kofun|ja|杵ガ森古墳}}) is a {{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} located in {{ill|Aizubange|ja|会津坂下町}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}. It is part of the {{ill|Unai Aotsu Kofun Group|ja|宇内青津古墳群}} and is designated as a Fukushima Prefecture Historic Site. Kinegamori Kofun is recognized as one of the oldest kofun in the Tohoku region. == Overview == Located west of the city center of Aizubange, along the western bank of the former Miyagawa River (Tsurunumagawa), this keyhole-shaped burial mound faces south. Surrounding it are other ancient burial mounds, including Usagamori Kofun and Maigamori Kofun (now disappeared), which illustrate the region's prosperity during the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. The rear section of Kinegamori Kofun is a two-tiered construction, while the details of the front section are unclear due to erosion. Its distinctive paddle-shaped design resembles that of the earliest keyhole-shaped mound, {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳}}, in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, though Kinegamori is about one-sixth the size of Hashihaka. The mound is encircled by a moat. The mound was first noted in the historical text ''Shinpen Aizu Fudoki'' as a mound, but it was officially identified during an archaeological survey in 1961. Before excavations, only part of the rear section was visible, with the remainder buried under rice fields. Excavations during 1990 revealed that the mound, previously thought to be circular, was actually a keyhole-shaped kofun. Additional findings included a surrounding moat, pit dwellings, and 10 {{ill|Square burial mounds|ja|方形周溝墓}}. Artifacts such as {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} suggest the mound was constructed in the late 3rd to early 4th century, making it one of the oldest kofun in Tohoku. == Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows: * Total length: 45.6 meters * Rear section: * Diameter: 25.6 meters * Height: ~2 meters * Front section: * Length: 24 meters * Width: 19 meters == Inarizuka Site == [[File:Inarizuka-iseki (Aizubange).JPG|thumb|200px|right|Inarizuka Site]] The '''Inarizuka Site''' ({{ill|Inarizuka Site|ja|稲荷塚遺跡}}) is an early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} site near Kinegamori Kofun. Excavations from 1990 to 1991 uncovered 15 pit dwellings, 3 {{ill|pillar-supported buildings|ja|掘立柱建物}}, and 10 square burial mounds. These findings suggest that the site initially functioned as a settlement during the early Kofun period. Kinegamori Kofun was later constructed on the site, with additional burial mounds built nearby for individuals associated with the kofun. In 2017, an additional 13 square burial mounds were discovered, bringing the total to 23, making it the largest collection of square burial mounds in Fukushima Prefecture[http://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20171122-222147.php "Fukushima Prefecture’s Largest 'Square Burial Mound Group' Discovered at Inarizuka Site"] (''Minyu'', November 22, 2017).. == Cultural Property == === Fukushima Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties === * Historic Site * Kinegamori Kofun - Designated on March 22, 1996[http://www.town.aizubange.fukushima.jp/soshiki/30/285.html "List of Cultural Properties in Aizubange"] (Aizubange Town website).. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == {{small|References used in this article}} * Historic Site Description Panel * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1993|chapter=Kinegamori Kofun|title={{ill|Historical Place names of Japan|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 7: Fukushima Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490077|ref={{Harvid|Kinegamori Kofun (Heibonsha)|1993}}}} * Hidehito Tsuji, *The Foundation of Tohoku Kofun Research: Aizu Otsukayama Kofun*, "Learning from Historical Sites" Series 029, Shinseisha, 2006. == Further Reading == {{small|Sources not directly used in the article}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1991|chapter=|title=Excavation Report on Kinegamori Kofun and Inarizuka Site (Aizubange Town Cultural Property Report No. 22)|publisher=Aizubange Town Board of Education|isbn=|ref=}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1995|chapter=|title=Preservation Report of Kinegamori Kofun (Aizubange Town Cultural Property Report No. 33)|publisher=Aizubange Town Board of Education|isbn=|ref=}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Kinegamori-kofun}} * [http://www.town.aizubange.fukushima.jp/soshiki/30/282.html Kinegamori Kofun] - {{Infobox ancient site |name=Kinegamori Kofun |image=[[File:Kinegamori-kofun zenkei.JPG|280px]]
Complete view of the burial mound (front section in foreground, rear section in back right) |group={{ill|Unai Aotsu Kofun Group|ja|宇内青津古墳群}} |location={{ill|Inarizuka, Aizubange|ja|稲荷塚}}, {{ill|Aizubange|ja|会津坂下町}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}} |coordinates={{Coord|37|33|58.73|N|139|48|34.89|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kinegamori Kofun}} |type={{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |size=45.6 m mound length
2 m height |constructed=Late 3rd to early 4th century |burial=Unknown |buried= |artifacts={{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} |cultural_property=Fukushima Prefecture Designated Historic Site: "Kinegamori Kofun" |notes=Possibly the oldest kofun in the Tohoku region }} '''Kinegamori Kofun''' ({{ill|Kinegamori Kofun|ja|杵ガ森古墳}}) is a {{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} located in {{ill|Aizubange|ja|会津坂下町}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}. It is part of the {{ill|Unai Aotsu Kofun Group|ja|宇内青津古墳群}} and is designated as a Fukushima Prefecture Historic Site. Kinegamori Kofun is recognized as one of the oldest kofun in the Tohoku region. == Overview == Located west of the city center of Aizubange, along the western bank of the former Miyagawa River (Tsurunumagawa), this keyhole-shaped burial mound faces south. Surrounding it are other ancient burial mounds, including Usagamori Kofun and Maigamori Kofun (now disappeared), which illustrate the region's prosperity during the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. The rear section of Kinegamori Kofun is a two-tiered construction, while the details of the front section are unclear due to erosion. Its distinctive paddle-shaped design resembles that of the earliest keyhole-shaped mound, {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳}}, in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, though Kinegamori is about one-sixth the size of Hashihaka. The mound is encircled by a moat. The mound was first noted in the historical text ''Shinpen Aizu Fudoki'' as a mound, but it was officially identified during an archaeological survey in 1961. Before excavations, only part of the rear section was visible, with the remainder buried under rice fields. Excavations during 1990 revealed that the mound, previously thought to be circular, was actually a keyhole-shaped kofun. Additional findings included a surrounding moat, pit dwellings, and 10 {{ill|Square burial mounds|ja|方形周溝墓}}. Artifacts such as {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} suggest the mound was constructed in the late 3rd to early 4th century, making it one of the oldest kofun in Tohoku. == Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows: * Total length: 45.6 meters * Rear section: * Diameter: 25.6 meters * Height: ~2 meters * Front section: * Length: 24 meters * Width: 19 meters == Inarizuka Site == [[File:Inarizuka-iseki (Aizubange).JPG|thumb|200px|right|Inarizuka Site]] The '''Inarizuka Site''' ({{ill|Inarizuka Site|ja|稲荷塚遺跡}}) is an early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} site near Kinegamori Kofun. Excavations from 1990 to 1991 uncovered 15 pit dwellings, 3 {{ill|pillar-supported buildings|ja|掘立柱建物}}, and 10 square burial mounds. These findings suggest that the site initially functioned as a settlement during the early Kofun period. Kinegamori Kofun was later constructed on the site, with additional burial mounds built nearby for individuals associated with the kofun. In 2017, an additional 13 square burial mounds were discovered, bringing the total to 23, making it the largest collection of square burial mounds in Fukushima Prefecture[http://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20171122-222147.php "Fukushima Prefecture’s Largest 'Square Burial Mound Group' Discovered at Inarizuka Site"] (''Minyu'', November 22, 2017).. == Cultural Property == === Fukushima Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties === * Historic Site * Kinegamori Kofun - Designated on March 22, 1996[http://www.town.aizubange.fukushima.jp/soshiki/30/285.html "List of Cultural Properties in Aizubange"] (Aizubange Town website).. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == {{small|References used in this article}} * Historic Site Description Panel * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1993|chapter=Kinegamori Kofun|title={{ill|Historical Place names of Japan|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 7: Fukushima Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490077|ref={{Harvid|Kinegamori Kofun (Heibonsha)|1993}}}} * Hidehito Tsuji, *The Foundation of Tohoku Kofun Research: Aizu Otsukayama Kofun*, "Learning from Historical Sites" Series 029, Shinseisha, 2006. == Further Reading == {{small|Sources not directly used in the article}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1991|chapter=|title=Excavation Report on Kinegamori Kofun and Inarizuka Site (Aizubange Town Cultural Property Report No. 22)|publisher=Aizubange Town Board of Education|isbn=|ref=}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1995|chapter=|title=Preservation Report of Kinegamori Kofun (Aizubange Town Cultural Property Report No. 33)|publisher=Aizubange Town Board of Education|isbn=|ref=}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Kinegamori-kofun}} * [http://www.town.aizubange.fukushima.jp/soshiki/30/282.html Kinegamori Kofun] - Aizubange Town website {{Archaeology}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Kofun in Fukushima Prefecture]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:History of Aizubange]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Fukushima Prefecture Designated Historic Sites]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:qq deprecated open]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Kinegamori Kofun]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:01-17]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:dd]][[Category:kk]][[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] {{Kofun navbar}} [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Kinegamori Kofun]] [[Category:SuQqqqqq]] [[Category:qqqq]] [[ja:杵ガ森古墳]] [[Category:null category]][[Category:new pages most likely untranslated or similar]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:Removing this excess]] [[Category:前方後円墳]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[Category:前方後円墳]] [[Category:前方後円墳]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[Category:前方後円墳]] [[Category:前方後円墳]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[Category:前方後円墳]] [[Category:前方後円墳]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[Category:前方後円墳]] [[Category:Completion tier 5]]

{{Infobox ancient site |name=Kinegamori Kofun |image=[[File:Kinegamori-kofun zenkei.JPG|280px]]
Complete view of the burial mound (front section in foreground, rear section in back right) |group={{ill|Unai Aotsu Kofun Group|ja|宇内青津古墳群}} |location={{ill|Inarizuka, Aizubange|ja|稲荷塚}}, {{ill|Aizubange|ja|会津坂下町}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}} |coordinates={{Coord|37|33|58.73|N|139|48|34.89|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kinegamori Kofun}} |type={{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |size=45.6 m mound length
2 m height |constructed=Late 3rd to early 4th century |burial=Unknown |buried= |artifacts={{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} |cultural_property=Fukushima Prefecture Designated Historic Site: "Kinegamori Kofun" |notes=Possibly the oldest kofun in the Tohoku region }} '''Kinegamori Kofun''' ({{ill|Kinegamori Kofun|ja|杵ガ森古墳}}) is a {{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} located in {{ill|Aizubange|ja|会津坂下町}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}. It is part of the {{ill|Unai Aotsu Kofun Group|ja|宇内青津古墳群}} and is designated as a Fukushima Prefecture Historic Site. Kinegamori Kofun is recognized as one of the oldest kofun in the Tohoku region. == Overview == Located west of the city center of Aizubange, along the western bank of the former Miyagawa River (Tsurunumagawa), this keyhole-shaped burial mound faces south. Surrounding it are other ancient burial mounds, including Usagamori Kofun and Maigamori Kofun (now disappeared), which illustrate the region's prosperity during the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. The rear section of Kinegamori Kofun is a two-tiered construction, while the details of the front section are unclear due to erosion. Its distinctive paddle-shaped design resembles that of the earliest keyhole-shaped mound, {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳}}, in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, though Kinegamori is about one-sixth the size of Hashihaka. The mound is encircled by a moat. The mound was first noted in the historical text ''Shinpen Aizu Fudoki'' as a mound, but it was officially identified during an archaeological survey in 1961. Before excavations, only part of the rear section was visible, with the remainder buried under rice fields. Excavations during 1990 revealed that the mound, previously thought to be circular, was actually a keyhole-shaped kofun. Additional findings included a surrounding moat, pit dwellings, and 10 {{ill|Square burial mounds|ja|方形周溝墓}}. Artifacts such as {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} suggest the mound was constructed in the late 3rd to early 4th century, making it one of the oldest kofun in Tohoku. == Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows: * Total length: 45.6 meters * Rear section: * Diameter: 25.6 meters * Height: ~2 meters * Front section: * Length: 24 meters * Width: 19 meters == Inarizuka Site == [[File:Inarizuka-iseki (Aizubange).JPG|thumb|200px|right|Inarizuka Site]] The '''Inarizuka Site''' ({{ill|Inarizuka Site|ja|稲荷塚遺跡}}) is an early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} site near Kinegamori Kofun. Excavations from 1990 to 1991 uncovered 15 pit dwellings, 3 {{ill|pillar-supported buildings|ja|掘立柱建物}}, and 10 square burial mounds. These findings suggest that the site initially functioned as a settlement during the early Kofun period. Kinegamori Kofun was later constructed on the site, with additional burial mounds built nearby for individuals associated with the kofun. In 2017, an additional 13 square burial mounds were discovered, bringing the total to 23, making it the largest collection of square burial mounds in Fukushima Prefecture[http://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20171122-222147.php "Fukushima Prefecture’s Largest 'Square Burial Mound Group' Discovered at Inarizuka Site"] (''Minyu'', November 22, 2017).. == Cultural Property == === Fukushima Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties === * Historic Site * Kinegamori Kofun - Designated on March 22, 1996[http://www.town.aizubange.fukushima.jp/soshiki/30/285.html "List of Cultural Properties in Aizubange"] (Aizubange Town website).. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == {{small|References used in this article}} * Historic Site Description Panel * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1993|chapter=Kinegamori Kofun|title={{ill|Historical Place names of Japan|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 7: Fukushima Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490077|ref={{Harvid|Kinegamori Kofun (Heibonsha)|1993}}}} * Hidehito Tsuji, *The Foundation of Tohoku Kofun Research: Aizu Otsukayama Kofun*, "Learning from Historical Sites" Series 029, Shinseisha, 2006. == Further Reading == {{small|Sources not directly used in the article}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1991|chapter=|title=Excavation Report on Kinegamori Kofun and Inarizuka Site (Aizubange Town Cultural Property Report No. 22)|publisher=Aizubange Town Board of Education|isbn=|ref=}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1995|chapter=|title=Preservation Report of Kinegamori Kofun (Aizubange Town Cultural Property Report No. 33)|publisher=Aizubange Town Board of Education|isbn=|ref=}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Kinegamori-kofun}} * [http://www.town.aizubange.fukushima.jp/soshiki/30/282.html Kinegamori Kofun] - 2 m height |constructed=Late 3rd to early 4th century |burial=Unknown |buried= |artifacts={{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} |cultural_property=Fukushima Prefecture Designated Historic Site: "Kinegamori Kofun" |notes=Possibly the oldest kofun in the Tohoku region }} '''Kinegamori Kofun''' ({{ill|Kinegamori Kofun|ja|杵ガ森古墳}}) is a {{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} located in {{ill|Aizubange|ja|会津坂下町}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}. It is part of the {{ill|Unai Aotsu Kofun Group|ja|宇内青津古墳群}} and is designated as a Fukushima Prefecture Historic Site. Kinegamori Kofun is recognized as one of the oldest kofun in the Tohoku region. == Overview == Located west of the city center of Aizubange, along the western bank of the former Miyagawa River (Tsurunumagawa), this keyhole-shaped burial mound faces south. Surrounding it are other ancient burial mounds, including Usagamori Kofun and Maigamori Kofun (now disappeared), which illustrate the region's prosperity during the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. The rear section of Kinegamori Kofun is a two-tiered construction, while the details of the front section are unclear due to erosion. Its distinctive paqqle-shaped design resembles that of the earliest keyhole-shaped mound, {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳}}, in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, though Kinegamori is about one-sixth the size of Hashihaka. The mound is encircled by a moat. The mound was first noted in the historical text ''Shinpen Aizu Fudoki'' as a mound, but it was officially identified during an archaeological survey in 1961. Before excavations, only part of the rear section was visible, with the remainder buried under rice fields. Excavations during 1990 revealed that the mound, previously thought to be circular, was actually a keyhole-shaped kofun. Aqqitional findings included a surrounding moat, pit dwellings, and 10 {{ill|Square burial mounds|ja|方形周溝墓}}. Artifacts such as {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} suggest the mound was constructed in the late 3rd to early 4th century, making it one of the oldest kofun in Tohoku. == Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows: * Total length: 45.6 meters * Rear section: * Diameter: 25.6 meters * Height: ~2 meters * Front section: * Length: 24 meters * Width: 19 meters == Inarizuka Site == [[File:Inarizuka-iseki (Aizubange).JPG|thumb|200px|right|Inarizuka Site]] The '''Inarizuka Site''' ({{ill|Inarizuka Site|ja|稲荷塚遺跡}}) is an early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} site near Kinegamori Kofun. Excavations from 1990 to 1991 uncovered 15 pit dwellings, 3 {{ill|pillar-supported buildings|ja|掘立柱建物}}, and 10 square burial mounds. These findings suggest that the site initially functioned as a settlement during the early Kofun period. Kinegamori Kofun was later constructed on the site, with aqqitional burial mounds built nearby for individuals associated with the kofun. In 2017, an aqqitional 13 square burial mounds were discovered, bringing the total to 23, making it the largest collection of square burial mounds in Fukushima Prefecture[http://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20171122-222147.php "Fukushima Prefecture’s Largest 'Square Burial Mound Group' Discovered at Inarizuka Site"] (''Minyu'', November 22, 2017).. == Cultural Property == === Fukushima Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties === * Historic Site * Kinegamori Kofun - Designated on March 22, 1996[http://www.town.aizubange.fukushima.jp/soshiki/30/285.html "List of Cultural Properties in Aizubange"] (Aizubange Town website).. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == {{small|References used in this article}} * Historic Site Description Panel * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1993|chapter=Kinegamori Kofun|title={{ill|Historical Place names of Japan|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 7: Fukushima Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490077|ref={{Harvid|Kinegamori Kofun (Heibonsha)|1993}}}} * Hidehito Tsuji, *The Foundation of Tohoku Kofun Research: Aizu Otsukayama Kofun*, "Learning from Historical Sites" Series 029, Shinseisha, 2006. == Further Reading == {{small|Sources not directly used in the article}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1991|chapter=|title=Excavation Report on Kinegamori Kofun and Inarizuka Site (Aizubange Town Cultural Property Report No. 22)|publisher=Aizubange Town Board of Education|isbn=|ref=}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1995|chapter=|title=Preservation Report of Kinegamori Kofun (Aizubange Town Cultural Property Report No. 33)|publisher=Aizubange Town Board of Education|isbn=|ref=}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Kinegamori-kofun}} * [http://www.town.aizubange.fukushima.jp/soshiki/30/282.html Kinegamori Kofun] - Designated on March 22, 1996[http://www.town.aizubange.fukushima.jp/soshiki/30/285.html "List of Cultural Properties in Aizubange"] (Aizubange Town website).. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == {{small|References used in this article}} * Historic Site Description Panel * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1993|chapter=Kinegamori Kofun|title={{ill|Historical Place names of Japan|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 7: Fukushima Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490077|ref={{Harvid|Kinegamori Kofun (Heibonsha)|1993}}}} * Hidehito Tsuji, *The Foundation of Tohoku Kofun Research: Aizu Otsukayama Kofun*, "Learning from Historical Sites" Series 029, Shinseisha, 2006. == Further Reading == {{small|Sources not directly used in the article}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1991|chapter=|title=Excavation Report on Kinegamori Kofun and Inarizuka Site (Aizubange Town Cultural Property Report No. 22)|publisher=Aizubange Town Board of Education|isbn=|ref=}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1995|chapter=|title=Preservation Report of Kinegamori Kofun (Aizubange Town Cultural Property Report No. 33)|publisher=Aizubange Town Board of Education|isbn=|ref=}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Kinegamori-kofun}} * [http://www.town.aizubange.fukushima.jp/soshiki/30/282.html Kinegamori Kofun] - {{Infobox ancient site |name=Kinegamori Kofun |image=[[File:Kinegamori-kofun zenkei.JPG|280px]]
Complete view of the burial mound (front section in foreground, rear section in back right) |group={{ill|Unai Aotsu Kofun Group|ja|宇内青津古墳群}} |location={{ill|Inarizuka, Aizubange|ja|稲荷塚}}, {{ill|Aizubange|ja|会津坂下町}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}} |coordinates={{Coord|37|33|58.73|N|139|48|34.89|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kinegamori Kofun}} |type={{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |size=45.6 m mound length
2 m height |constructed=Late 3rd to early 4th century |burial=Unknown |buried= |artifacts={{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} |cultural_property=Fukushima Prefecture Designated Historic Site: "Kinegamori Kofun" |notes=Possibly the oldest kofun in the Tohoku region }} '''Kinegamori Kofun''' ({{ill|Kinegamori Kofun|ja|杵ガ森古墳}}) is a {{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} located in {{ill|Aizubange|ja|会津坂下町}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}. It is part of the {{ill|Unai Aotsu Kofun Group|ja|宇内青津古墳群}} and is designated as a Fukushima Prefecture Historic Site. Kinegamori Kofun is recognized as one of the oldest kofun in the Tohoku region. == Overview == Located west of the city center of Aizubange, along the western bank of the former Miyagawa River (Tsurunumagawa), this keyhole-shaped burial mound faces south. Surrounding it are other ancient burial mounds, including Usagamori Kofun and Maigamori Kofun (now disappeared), which illustrate the region's prosperity during the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. The rear section of Kinegamori Kofun is a two-tiered construction, while the details of the front section are unclear due to erosion. Its distinctive paqqle-shaped design resembles that of the earliest keyhole-shaped mound, {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳}}, in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, though Kinegamori is about one-sixth the size of Hashihaka. The mound is encircled by a moat. The mound was first noted in the historical text ''Shinpen Aizu Fudoki'' as a mound, but it was officially identified during an archaeological survey in 1961. Before excavations, only part of the rear section was visible, with the remainder buried under rice fields. Excavations during 1990 revealed that the mound, previously thought to be circular, was actually a keyhole-shaped kofun. Aqqitional findings included a surrounding moat, pit dwellings, and 10 {{ill|Square burial mounds|ja|方形周溝墓}}. Artifacts such as {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} suggest the mound was constructed in the late 3rd to early 4th century, making it one of the oldest kofun in Tohoku. == Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows: * Total length: 45.6 meters * Rear section: * Diameter: 25.6 meters * Height: ~2 meters * Front section: * Length: 24 meters * Width: 19 meters == Inarizuka Site == [[File:Inarizuka-iseki (Aizubange).JPG|thumb|200px|right|Inarizuka Site]] The '''Inarizuka Site''' ({{ill|Inarizuka Site|ja|稲荷塚遺跡}}) is an early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} site near Kinegamori Kofun. Excavations from 1990 to 1991 uncovered 15 pit dwellings, 3 {{ill|pillar-supported buildings|ja|掘立柱建物}}, and 10 square burial mounds. These findings suggest that the site initially functioned as a settlement during the early Kofun period. Kinegamori Kofun was later constructed on the site, with aqqitional burial mounds built nearby for individuals associated with the kofun. In 2017, an aqqitional 13 square burial mounds were discovered, bringing the total to 23, making it the largest collection of square burial mounds in Fukushima Prefecture[http://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20171122-222147.php "Fukushima Prefecture’s Largest 'Square Burial Mound Group' Discovered at Inarizuka Site"] (''Minyu'', November 22, 2017).. == Cultural Property == === Fukushima Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties === * Historic Site * Kinegamori Kofun - Designated on March 22, 1996[http://www.town.aizubange.fukushima.jp/soshiki/30/285.html "List of Cultural Properties in Aizubange"] (Aizubange Town website).. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == {{small|References used in this article}} * Historic Site Description Panel * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1993|chapter=Kinegamori Kofun|title={{ill|Historical Place names of Japan|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 7: Fukushima Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490077|ref={{Harvid|Kinegamori Kofun (Heibonsha)|1993}}}} * Hidehito Tsuji, *The Foundation of Tohoku Kofun Research: Aizu Otsukayama Kofun*, "Learning from Historical Sites" Series 029, Shinseisha, 2006. == Further Reading == {{small|Sources not directly used in the article}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1991|chapter=|title=Excavation Report on Kinegamori Kofun and Inarizuka Site (Aizubange Town Cultural Property Report No. 22)|publisher=Aizubange Town Board of Education|isbn=|ref=}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1995|chapter=|title=Preservation Report of Kinegamori Kofun (Aizubange Town Cultural Property Report No. 33)|publisher=Aizubange Town Board of Education|isbn=|ref=}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Kinegamori-kofun}} * [http://www.town.aizubange.fukushima.jp/soshiki/30/282.html Kinegamori Kofun] - Aizubange Town website {{Archaeology}} [[Category:kk]][[Category:qq]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Kofun in Fukushima Prefecture]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:qq]][[Category:kk]][[Category:History of Aizubange]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:qq]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:qq]][[Category:kk]][[Category:Fukushima Prefecture Designated Historic Sites]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:qq]][[Category:kk]][[Category:qq deprecated open]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:qq]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:qq]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:qq]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]][[Category:qq]][[Category:kk]][[Category:01-17]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:qq]][[Category:kk]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] {{Kofun navbar}} [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]] [[ja:杵ガ森古墳]] [[Category:new pages most likely untranslated or similar]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[Category:qq]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[Category:qq]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kinegamori Kofun}}

{{Infobox ancient site |name=Kinegamori Kofun |image=[[File:Kinegamori-kofun zenkei.JPG|280px]]
Complete view of the burial mound (front section in foreground, rear section in back right) |group={{ill|Unai Aotsu Kofun Group|ja|宇内青津古墳群}} |location={{ill|Inarizuka, Aizubange|ja|稲荷塚}}, {{ill|Aizubange|ja|会津坂下町}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}} |coordinates={{Coord|37|33|58.73|N|139|48|34.89|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kinegamori Kofun}} |type={{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |size=45.6 m mound length
2 m height |constructed=Late 3rd to early 4th century |burial=Unknown |buried= |artifacts={{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} |cultural_property=Fukushima Prefecture Designated Historic Site: "Kinegamori Kofun" |notes=Possibly the oldest kofun in the Tohoku region }} '''Kinegamori Kofun''' ({{ill|Kinegamori Kofun|ja|杵ガ森古墳}}) is a {{ill|Keyhole-shaped burial mound|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} located in {{ill|Aizubange|ja|会津坂下町}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}. It is part of the {{ill|Unai Aotsu Kofun Group|ja|宇内青津古墳群}} and is designated as a Fukushima Prefecture Historic Site. Kinegamori Kofun is recognized as one of the oldest kofun in the Tohoku region. == Overview == Located west of the city center of Aizubange, along the western bank of the former Miyagawa River (Tsurunumagawa), this keyhole-shaped burial mound faces south. Surrounding it are other ancient burial mounds, including Usagamori Kofun and Maigamori Kofun (now disappeared), which illustrate the region's prosperity during the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. The rear section of Kinegamori Kofun is a two-tiered construction, while the details of the front section are unclear due to erosion. Its distinctive paqqle-shaped design resembles that of the earliest keyhole-shaped mound, {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳}}, in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, though Kinegamori is about one-sixth the size of Hashihaka. The mound is encircled by a moat. The mound was first noted in the historical text ''Shinpen Aizu Fudoki'' as a mound, but it was officially identified during an archaeological survey in 1961. Before excavations, only part of the rear section was visible, with the remainder buried under rice fields. Excavations during 1990 revealed that the mound, previously thought to be circular, was actually a keyhole-shaped kofun. Aqqitional findings included a surrounding moat, pit dwellings, and 10 {{ill|Square burial mounds|ja|方形周溝墓}}. Artifacts such as {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} suggest the mound was constructed in the late 3rd to early 4th century, making it one of the oldest kofun in Tohoku. == Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows: * Total length: 45.6 meters * Rear section: * Diameter: 25.6 meters * Height: ~2 meters * Front section: * Length: 24 meters * Width: 19 meters == Inarizuka Site == [[File:Inarizuka-iseki (Aizubange).JPG|thumb|200px|right|Inarizuka Site]] The '''Inarizuka Site''' ({{ill|Inarizuka Site|ja|稲荷塚遺跡}}) is an early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} site near Kinegamori Kofun. Excavations from 1990 to 1991 uncovered 15 pit dwellings, 3 {{ill|pillar-supported buildings|ja|掘立柱建物}}, and 10 square burial mounds. These findings suggest that the site initially functioned as a settlement during the early Kofun period. Kinegamori Kofun was later constructed on the site, with aqqitional burial mounds built nearby for individuals associated with the kofun. In 2017, an aqqitional 13 square burial mounds were discovered, bringing the total to 23, making it the largest collection of square burial mounds in Fukushima Prefecture[http://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20171122-222147.php "Fukushima Prefecture’s Largest 'Square Kofun Group' Discovered at Inarizuka Site"] (''Minyu'', November 22, 2017).. == Cultural Property == === Fukushima Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties === * Historic Site * Kinegamori Kofun - Designated on March 22, 1996[http://www.town.aizubange.fukushima.jp/soshiki/30/285.html "List of Cultural Properties in Aizubange"] (Aizubange Town website).. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == {{small|References used in this article}} * Historic Site Description Panel * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1993|chapter=Kinegamori Kofun|title={{ill|Historical Place names of Japan|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 7: Fukushima Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490077|ref={{Harvid|Kinegamori Kofun (Heibonsha)|1993}}}} * Hidehito Tsuji, *The Foundation of Tohoku Kofun Research: Aizu Otsukayama Kofun*, "Learning from Historical Sites" Series 029, Shinseisha, 2006. == Further Reading == {{small|Sources not directly used in the article}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1991|chapter=|title=Excavation Report on Kinegamori Kofun and Inarizuka Site (Aizubange Town Cultural Property Report No. 22)|publisher=Aizubange Town Board of Education|isbn=|ref=}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1995|chapter=|title=Preservation Report of Kinegamori Kofun (Aizubange Town Cultural Property Report No. 33)|publisher=Aizubange Town Board of Education|isbn=|ref=}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Kinegamori-kofun}} * [http://www.town.aizubange.fukushima.jp/soshiki/30/282.html Kinegamori Kofun] - The mound is encircled by a moat. The mound was first noted in the historical text ''Shinpen Aizu Fudoki'' as a mound, but it was officially identified during an archaeological survey in 1961. Before excavations, only part of the rear section was visible, with the remainder buried under rice fields. Excavations during 1990 revealed that the mound, previously thought to be circular, was actually a keyhole-shaped kofun. A11itional findings included a surrounding moat, pit dwellings, and 10 {{ill|Square Kofuns|ja|方形周溝墓}}. Artifacts such as {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} suggest the mound was constructed in the late 3rd to early 4th century, making it one of the oldest kofun in Tohoku. == Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows: * Total length: 45.6 meters * Rear section: * Diameter: 25.6 meters * Height: ~2 meters * Front section: * Length: 24 meters * Width: 19 meters == Inarizuka Site == [[File:Inarizuka-iseki (Aizubange).JPG|thumb|200px|right|Inarizuka Site]] The '''Inarizuka Site''' ({{ill|Inarizuka Site|ja|稲荷塚遺跡}}) is an early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} site near Kinegamori Kofun. Excavations from 1990 to 1991 uncovered 15 pit dwellings, 3 {{ill|pillar-supported buildings|ja|掘立柱建物}}, and 10 square Kofuns. These findings suggest that the site initially functioned as a settlement during the early Kofun period. Kinegamori Kofun was later constructed on the site, with a11itional Kofuns built nearby for individuals associated with the kofun. In 2017, an a11itional 13 square Kofuns were discovered, bringing the total to 23, making it the largest collection of square Kofuns in Fukushima Prefecture[http://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20171122-222147.php "Fukushima Prefecture’s Largest 'Square Kofun Group' Discovered at Inarizuka Site"] (''Minyu'', November 22, 2017).. == Cultural Property == === Fukushima Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties === * Historic Site * Kinegamori Kofun - {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] |name=Kinegamori Kofun |image=[[File:Kinegamori-kofun zenkei.JPG|280px]]
Complete view of the Kofun (front section in foreground, rear section in back right) |group={{ill|Unai Aotsu Kofun Group|ja|宇内青津古墳群}} |location={{ill|Inarizuka, Aizubange|ja|稲荷塚}}, {{ill|Aizubange|ja|会津坂下町}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}} |coordinates={{Coord|37|33|58.73|N|139|48|34.89|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kinegamori Kofun}} |type={{ill|Keyhole-shaped Kofun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |size=45.6 m mound length
2 m height |constructed=Late 3rd to early 4th century |burial=Unknown |buried= |artifacts={{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} |cultural_property=Fukushima Prefecture Designated Historic Site: "Kinegamori Kofun" |notes=Possibly the oldest kofun in the Tohoku region }} '''Kinegamori Kofun''' ({{ill|Kinegamori Kofun|ja|杵ガ森古墳}}) is a {{ill|Keyhole-shaped Kofun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} located in {{ill|Aizubange|ja|会津坂下町}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}. It is part of the {{ill|Unai Aotsu Kofun Group|ja|宇内青津古墳群}} and is designated as a Fukushima Prefecture Historic Site. Kinegamori Kofun is recognized as one of the oldest kofun in the Tohoku region. == Overview == Located west of the city center of Aizubange, along the western bank of the former Miyagawa River (Tsurunumagawa), this keyhole-shaped Kofun faces south. Surrounding it are other ancient Kofuns, including Usagamori Kofun and Maigamori Kofun (now disappeared), which illustrate the region's prosperity during the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. The rear section of Kinegamori Kofun is a two-tiered construction, while the details of the front section are unclear due to erosion. Its distinctive pa11le-shaped design resembles that of the earliest keyhole-shaped mound, {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳}}, in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, though Kinegamori is about one-sixth the size of Hashihaka. The mound is encircled by a moat. The mound was first noted in the historical text ''Shinpen Aizu Fudoki'' as a mound, but it was officially identified during an archaeological survey in 1961. Before excavations, only part of the rear section was visible, with the remainder buried under rice fields. Excavations during 1990 revealed that the mound, previously thought to be circular, was actually a keyhole-shaped kofun. A11itional findings included a surrounding moat, pit dwellings, and 10 {{ill|Square Kofuns|ja|方形周溝墓}}. Artifacts such as {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} suggest the mound was constructed in the late 3rd to early 4th century, making it one of the oldest kofun in Tohoku. == Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows: * Total length: 45.6 meters * Rear section: ** Diameter: 25.6 meters ** Height: ~2 meters * Front section: ** Length: 24 meters ** Width: 19 meters == Inarizuka Site == [[File:Inarizuka-iseki (Aizubange).JPG|thumb|200px|right|Inarizuka Site]] The '''Inarizuka Site''' ({{ill|Inarizuka Site|ja|稲荷塚遺跡}}) is an early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} site near Kinegamori Kofun. Excavations from 1990 to 1991 uncovered 15 pit dwellings, 3 {{ill|pillar-supported buildings|ja|掘立柱建物}}, and 10 square Kofuns. These findings suggest that the site initially functioned as a settlement during the early Kofun period. Kinegamori Kofun was later constructed on the site, with a11ition[[Category:a11ition]]al Kofuns built nearby for individuals associated with the kofun. In 2017, an a11ition[[Category:a11ition]]al 13 square Kofuns were discovered, bringing the total to 23, making it the largest collection of square Kofuns in Fukushima Prefecture[http://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM20171122-222147.php "Fukushima Prefecture’s Largest 'Square Kofun Group' Discovered at Inarizuka Site"] (''Minyu'', November 22, 2017).. == Cultural Property == === Fukushima Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties === * Historic Site ** Kinegamori Kofun - Designated on March 22, 1996[http://www.town.aizubange.fukushima.jp/soshiki/30/285.html "List of Cultural Properties in Aizubange"] (Aizubange Town website).. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == {{small|References used in this article}} * Historic Site Description Panel * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1993|chapter=Kinegamori Kofun|title={{ill|Historical Place names of Japan|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 7: Fukushima Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490077|ref={{Harvid|Kinegamori Kofun (Heibonsha)|1993}}}} * Hidehito Tsuji, *The Foundation of Tohoku Kofun Research: Aizu Otsukayama Kofun*, "Learning from Historical Sites" Series 029, Shinseisha, 2006. == Further Reading == {{small|Sources not directly used in the article}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1991|chapter=|title=Excavation Report on Kinegamori Kofun and Inarizuka Site (Aizubange Town Cultural Property Report No. 22)|publisher=Aizubange Town Board of Education|isbn=|ref=}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1995|chapter=|title=Preservation Report of Kinegamori Kofun (Aizubange Town Cultural Property Report No. 33)|publisher=Aizubange Town Board of Education|isbn=|ref=}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Kinegamori-kofun}} * [http://www.town.aizubange.fukushima.jp/soshiki/30/282.html Kinegamori Kofun] - Aizubange Town website {{Archaeology}} [[Category:11]][[Category:11]][[Category:11]][[Category:Kofun in Fukushima Prefecture]] [[Category:11]][[Category:11]][[Category:11]][[Category:History of Aizubange]] [[Category:11]][[Category:11]][[Category:11]][[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:11]][[Category:11]][[Category:11]][[Category:Fukushima Prefecture Designated Historic Sites]] [[Category:11]][[Category:11]][[Category:11]][[Category:11 deprecated open]] [[Category:11]][[Category:11]][[Category:11]][[Category:11]][[Category:11]][[Category:11]][[Category:11]][[Category:11]][[Category:11]][[Category:11]][[Category:11]][[Category:11]][[Category:01-17]] [[Category:11]][[Category:11]][[Category:11]][[Category:11]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] {{Kofun navbar}} [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:11]] [[ja:杵ガ森古墳]] [[Category:goodbye $1 most likely untranslated or similar]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[Category:11]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[Category:Completion tier 5]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[Category:11]] [[Category:11]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[Category:11]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kinegamori Kofun}} [[Category:New]]

Complete view of the Kofun (front section in foreground, rear section in back right) |group={{ill|Unai Aotsu Kofun Group|ja|宇内青津古墳群}} |location={{ill|Inarizuka, Aizubange|ja|稲荷塚}}, {{ill|Aizubange|ja|会津坂下町}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}} |coordinates={{Coord|37|33|58.73|N|139|48|34.89|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kinegamori Kofun}} |type={{ill|Keyhole-shaped Kofun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |size=45.6 m mound length
2 m height |constructed=Late 3rd to early 4th century |burial=Unknown |buried= |artifacts={{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} |cultural_property=Fukushima Prefecture Designated Historic Site: "Kinegamori Kofun" |notes=Possibly the oldest kofun in the Tohoku region }} '''Kinegamori Kofun''' ({{ill|Kinegamori Kofun|ja|杵ガ森古墳}}) is a {{ill|Keyhole-shaped Kofun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} located in {{ill|Aizubange|ja|会津坂下町}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}. It is part of the {{ill|Unai Aotsu Kofun Group|ja|宇内青津古墳群}} and is designated as a Fukushima Prefecture Historic Site. Kinegamori Kofun is recognized as one of the oldest kofun in the Tohoku region. == Overview == Located west of the city center of Aizubange, along the western bank of the former Miyagawa River (Tsurunumagawa), this keyhole-shaped Kofun faces south. Surrounding it are other ancient Kofuns, including Usagamori Kofun and Maigamori Kofun (now disappeared), which illustrate the region's prosperity during the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. The rear section of Kinegamori Kofun is a two-tiered construction, while the details of the front section are unclear due to erosion. Its distinctive paddle-shaped design resembles that of the earliest keyhole-shaped mound, {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳}}, in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, though Kinegamori is about one-sixth the size of Hashihaka. The mound is encircled by a moat. The mound was first noted in the historical text ''Shinpen Aizu Fudoki'' as a mound, but it was officially identified during an archaeological survey in 1961. Before excavations, only part of the rear section was visible, with the remainder buried under rice fields. Excavations during 1990 revealed that the mound, previously thought to be circular, was actually a keyhole-shaped kofun. Additional findings included a surrounding moat, pit dwellings, and 10 {{ill|Square Kofuns|ja|方形周溝墓}}. Artifacts such as {{ill|Haji ware|ja|土師器}} suggest the mound was constructed in the late 3rd to early 4th century, making it one of the oldest kofun in Tohoku. == Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows: * Total length: 45.6 meters * Rear section: ** Diameter: 25.6 meters ** Height: ~2 meters * Front section: ** Length: 24 meters ** Width: 19 meters == Inarizuka Site == [[File:Inarizuka-iseki (Aizubange).JPG|thumb|200px|right|Inarizuka Site]] The '''Inarizuka Site''' ({{ill|Inarizuka Site|ja|稲荷塚遺跡}}) is an early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} site near Kinegamori Kofun. Excavations from 1990 to 1991 uncovered 15 pit dwellings, 3 {{ill|pillar-supported buildings|ja|掘立柱建物}}, and 10 square Kofuns. These findings suggest that the site initially functioned as a settlement during the early Kofun period. Kinegamori Kofun was later constructed on the site, with addition[[Category:addition]]al Kofuns built nearby for individuals associated with the kofun. In 2017, an addition[[Category:addition]]al 13 square Kofuns were discovered, bringing the total to 23, making it the largest collection of square Kofuns in Fukushima Prefecture[http://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM2017dd22-222147.php "Fukushima Prefecture’s Largest 'Square Kofun Group' Discovered at Inarizuka Site"] (''Minyu'', November 22, 2017).. == Cultural Property == === Fukushima Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties === * Historic Site ** Kinegamori Kofun - Designated on March 22, 1996[http://www.town.aizubange.fukushima.jp/soshiki/30/285.html "List of Cultural Properties in Aizubange"] (Aizubange Town website).. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == {{small|References used in this article}} * Historic Site Description Panel * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1993|chapter=Kinegamori Kofun|title={{ill|Historical Place names of Japan|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 7: Fukushima Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490077|ref={{Harvid|Kinegamori Kofun (Heibonsha)|1993}}}} * Hidehito Tsuji, *The Foundation of Tohoku Kofun Research: Aizu Otsukayama Kofun*, "Learning from Historical Sites" Series 029, Shinseisha, 2006. == Further Reading == {{small|Sources not directly used in the article}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1991|chapter=|title=Excavation Report on Kinegamori Kofun and Inarizuka Site (Aizubange Town Cultural Property Report No. 22)|publisher=Aizubange Town Board of Education|isbn=|ref=}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1995|chapter=|title=Preservation Report of Kinegamori Kofun (Aizubange Town Cultural Property Report No. 33)|publisher=Aizubange Town Board of Education|isbn=|ref=}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Kinegamori-kofun}} * [http://www.town.aizubange.fukushima.jp/soshiki/30/282.html Kinegamori Kofun] - Kinegamori Kofun is recognized as one of the oldest kofun in the Tohoku region. == Overview == Located west of the city center of Aizubange, along the western bank of the former Miyagawa River (Tsurunumagawa), this keyhole-shaped Kofun faces south. Surrounding it are other ancient Kofuns, including Usagamori Kofun and Maigamori Kofun (now disappeared), which illustrate the region's prosperity during the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. The rear section of Kinegamori Kofun is a two-tiered construction, while the details of the front section are unclear due to erosion. Its distinctive paddle-shaped design resembles that of the earliest keyhole-shaped mound, {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳}}, in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, though Kinegamori is about one-sixth the size of Hashihaka. The mound is encircled by a moat. The mound was first noted in the historical text ''Shinpen Aizu Fudoki'' as a mound, but it was officially identified during an archaeological survey in 1961. Before excavations, only part of the rear section was visible, with the remainder buried under rice fields. Excavations during 1990 revealed that the mound, previously thought to be circular, was actually a keyhole-shaped kofun. Additional findings included a surrounding moat, pit dwellings, and 10 {{ill|Square Kofuns|ja|方形周溝墓}}. Artifacts such as {{ill|Haji pottery|ja|土師器}} suggest the mound was constructed in the late 3rd to early 4th century, making it one of the oldest kofun in Tohoku. == Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows: * Total length: 45.6 meters * Rear section: ** Diameter: 25.6 meters ** Height: ~2 meters * Front section: ** Length: 24 meters ** Width: 19 meters == Inarizuka Site == [[File:Inarizuka-iseki (Aizubange).JPG|thumb|200px|right|Inarizuka Site]] The '''Inarizuka Site''' ({{ill|Inarizuka Site|ja|稲荷塚遺跡}}) is an early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} site near Kinegamori Kofun. Excavations from 1990 to 1991 uncovered 15 pit dwellings, 3 {{ill|pillar-supported buildings|ja|掘立柱建物}}, and 10 square Kofuns. These findings suggest that the site initially functioned as a settlement during the early Kofun period. Kinegamori Kofun was later constructed on the site, with addition[[Category:addition]]al Kofuns built nearby for individuals associated with the kofun. In 2017, an addition[[Category:addition]]al 13 square Kofuns were discovered, bringing the total to 23, making it the largest collection of square Kofuns in Fukushima Prefecture[http://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM2017dd22-222147.php "Fukushima Prefecture’s Largest 'Square Kofun Group' Discovered at Inarizuka Site"] (''Minyu'', November 22, 2017).. == Cultural Property == === Fukushima Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties === * Historic Site ** Kinegamori Kofun - Designated on March 22, 1996[http://www.town.aizubange.fukushima.jp/soshiki/30/285.html "List of Cultural Properties in Aizubange"] (Aizubange Town website).. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == {{small|References used in this article}} * Historic Site Description Panel * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1993|chapter=Kinegamori Kofun|title={{ill|Historical Place names of Japan|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 7: Fukushima Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490077|ref={{Harvid|Kinegamori Kofun (Heibonsha)|1993}}}} * Hidehito Tsuji, *The Foundation of Tohoku Kofun Research: Aizu Otsukayama Kofun*, "Learning from Historical Sites" Series 029, Shinseisha, 2006. == Further Reading == {{small|Sources not directly used in the article}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1991|chapter=|title=Excavation Report on Kinegamori Kofun and Inarizuka Site (Aizubange Town Cultural Property Report No. 22)|publisher=Aizubange Town Board of Education|isbn=|ref=}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1995|chapter=|title=Preservation Report of Kinegamori Kofun (Aizubange Town Cultural Property Report No. 33)|publisher=Aizubange Town Board of Education|isbn=|ref=}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Kinegamori-kofun}} * [http://www.town.aizubange.fukushima.jp/soshiki/30/282.html Kinegamori Kofun] - Excavations from 1990 to 1991 uncovered 15 pit dwellings, 3 {{ill|pillar-supported buildings|ja|掘立柱建物}}, and 10 square Kofuns. These findings suggest that the site initially functioned as a settlement during the early Kofun period. Kinegamori Kofun was later constructed on the site, with addition[[Category:addition]]al Kofuns built nearby for individuals associated with the kofun. In 2017, an addition[[Category:addition]]al 13 square Kofuns were discovered, bringing the total to 23, making it the largest collection of square Kofuns in Fukushima Prefecture[http://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM2017dd22-222147.php "Fukushima Prefecture’s Largest 'Square Kofun Group' Discovered at Inarizuka Site"] (''Minyu'', November 22, 2017).. == Cultural Property == === Fukushima Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties === * Historic Site ** Kinegamori Kofun - Designated on March 22, 1996[http://www.town.aizubange.fukushima.jp/soshiki/30/285.html "List of Cultural Properties in Aizubange"] (Aizubange Town website).. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == {{small|References used in this article}} * Historic Site Description Panel * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1993|chapter=Kinegamori Kofun|title={{ill|Historical Place names of Japan|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 7: Fukushima Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490077|ref={{Harvid|Kinegamori Kofun (Heibonsha)|1993}}}} * Hidehito Tsuji, *The Foundation of Tohoku Kofun Research: Aizu Otsukayama Kofun*, "Learning from Historical Sites" Series 029, Shinseisha, 2006. == Further Reading == {{small|Sources not directly used in the article}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1991|chapter=|title=Excavation Report on Kinegamori Kofun and Inarizuka Site (Aizubange Town Cultural Property Report No. 22)|publisher=Aizubange Town Board of Education|isbn=|ref=}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1995|chapter=|title=Preservation Report of Kinegamori Kofun (Aizubange Town Cultural Property Report No. 33)|publisher=Aizubange Town Board of Education|isbn=|ref=}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Kinegamori-kofun}} * [http://www.town.aizubange.fukushima.jp/soshiki/30/282.html Kinegamori Kofun] - {{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] |name=Kinegamori Kofun |image=[[File:Kinegamori-kofun zenkei.JPG|280px]]
Complete view of the Kofun (front section in foreground, rear section in back right) |group={{ill|Unai Aotsu Kofun Group|ja|宇内青津古墳群}} |location={{ill|Inarizuka, Aizubange|ja|稲荷塚}}, {{ill|Aizubange|ja|会津坂下町}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}} |coordinates={{Coord|37|33|58.73|N|139|48|34.89|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kinegamori Kofun}} |type={{ill|Keyhole-shaped Kofun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |size=45.6 m mound length
2 m height |constructed=Late 3rd to early 4th century |burial=Unknown |buried= |artifacts={{ill|Haji pottery|ja|土師器}} |cultural_property=Fukushima Prefecture Designated Historic Site: "Kinegamori Kofun" |notes=Possibly the oldest kofun in the Tohoku region }} '''Kinegamori Kofun''' ({{ill|Kinegamori Kofun|ja|杵ガ森古墳}}) is a {{ill|Keyhole-shaped Kofun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} located in {{ill|Aizubange|ja|会津坂下町}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}. It is part of the {{ill|Unai Aotsu Kofun Group|ja|宇内青津古墳群}} and is designated as a Fukushima Prefecture Historic Site. Kinegamori Kofun is recognized as one of the oldest kofun in the Tohoku region. == Overview == Located west of the city center of Aizubange, along the western bank of the former Miyagawa River (Tsurunumagawa), this keyhole-shaped Kofun faces south. Surrounding it are other ancient Kofuns, including Usagamori Kofun and Maigamori Kofun (now disappeared), which illustrate the region's prosperity during the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. The rear section of Kinegamori Kofun is a two-tiered construction, while the details of the front section are unclear due to erosion. Its distinctive paddle-shaped design resembles that of the earliest keyhole-shaped mound, {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳}}, in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, though Kinegamori is about one-sixth the size of Hashihaka. The mound is encircled by a moat. The mound was first noted in the historical text ''Shinpen Aizu Fudoki'' as a mound, but it was officially identified during an archaeological survey in 1961. Before excavations, only part of the rear section was visible, with the remainder buried under rice fields. Excavations during 1990 revealed that the mound, previously thought to be circular, was actually a keyhole-shaped kofun. Additional findings included a surrounding moat, pit dwellings, and 10 {{ill|Square Kofuns|ja|方形周溝墓}}. Artifacts such as {{ill|Haji pottery|ja|土師器}} suggest the mound was constructed in the late 3rd to early 4th century, making it one of the oldest kofun in Tohoku. == Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows: * Total length: 45.6 meters * Rear section: ** Diameter: 25.6 meters ** Height: ~2 meters * Front section: ** Length: 24 meters ** Width: 19 meters == Inarizuka Site == [[File:Inarizuka-iseki (Aizubange).JPG|thumb|200px|right|Inarizuka Site]] The '''Inarizuka Site''' ({{ill|Inarizuka Site|ja|稲荷塚遺跡}}) is an early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} site near Kinegamori Kofun. Excavations from 1990 to 1991 uncovered 15 pit dwellings, 3 {{ill|pillar-supported buildings|ja|掘立柱建物}}, and 10 square Kofuns. These findings suggest that the site initially functioned as a settlement during the early Kofun period. Kinegamori Kofun was later constructed on the site, with addition[[Category:addition]]al Kofuns built nearby for individuals associated with the kofun. In 2017, an addition[[Category:addition]]al 13 square Kofuns were discovered, bringing the total to 23, making it the largest collection of square Kofuns in Fukushima Prefecture[http://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM2017dd22-222147.php "Fukushima Prefecture’s Largest 'Square Kofun Group' Discovered at Inarizuka Site"] (''Minyu'', November 22, 2017).. == Cultural Property == === Fukushima Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties === * Historic Site ** Kinegamori Kofun - Designated on March 22, 1996[http://www.town.aizubange.fukushima.jp/soshiki/30/285.html "List of Cultural Properties in Aizubange"] (Aizubange Town website).. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == {{small|References used in this article}} * Historic Site Description Panel * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1993|chapter=Kinegamori Kofun|title={{ill|Historical Place names of Japan|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 7: Fukushima Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490077|ref={{Harvid|Kinegamori Kofun (Heibonsha)|1993}}}} * Hidehito Tsuji, *The Foundation of Tohoku Kofun Research: Aizu Otsukayama Kofun*, "Learning from Historical Sites" Series 029, Shinseisha, 2006. == Further Reading == {{small|Sources not directly used in the article}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1991|chapter=|title=Excavation Report on Kinegamori Kofun and Inarizuka Site (Aizubange Town Cultural Property Report No. 22)|publisher=Aizubange Town Board of Education|isbn=|ref=}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1995|chapter=|title=Preservation Report of Kinegamori Kofun (Aizubange Town Cultural Property Report No. 33)|publisher=Aizubange Town Board of Education|isbn=|ref=}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Kinegamori-kofun}} * [http://www.town.aizubange.fukushima.jp/soshiki/30/282.html Kinegamori Kofun] - Aizubange Town website {{Archaeology}} [[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:Kofun in Fukushima Prefecture]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:History of Aizubange]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:Fukushima Prefecture Designated Historic Sites]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:dd deprecated open]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:01-17]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:dd]] [[ja:杵ガ森古墳]] [[Category:dd]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[Category:dd]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[Category:dd]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[Category:dd]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[Category:dd]] [[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[Category:Completion tier 6]][[Category: tier 6]][[Category:dd]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:dd]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[Category:dd]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kinegamori Kofun}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] {{Kofun navbar}}

{{afc comment|1=I hope this is not too annoying but this article is gonna be placed under [[:Category:Pages using infobox ancient site with unknown parameters]] and there is a decent chance these unknown parameters are ones[[Category:marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]] I want added to the main template like burial chamber structure and burial goods. Please do not use any bots that just remove the unknown parameters without looking at them. This was added through [[Special:MassEditRegex]] to all pages with the category so apologies if it was just some kind of an error like the image parameter being in Japanese[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 06:00, 27 March 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = [[Zenpokoenfun]] |name=Kinegamori Kofun |image=[[File:Kinegamori-kofun zenkei.JPG|280px]]
Complete view of the Kofun (front section in foreground, rear section in back right) |group={{ill|Unai Aotsu Kofun Group|ja|宇内青津古墳群}} |location={{ill|Inarizuka, Aizubange|ja|稲荷塚}}, {{ill|Aizubange|ja|会津坂下町}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}} |coordinates={{Coord|37|33|58.73|N|139|48|34.89|E|region:JP-07_type:landmark|display=inline,title|name=Kinegamori Kofun}} |type={{ill|Keyhole-shaped Kofun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} |size=45.6 m mound length
2 m height |constructed=Late 3rd to early 4th century |burial=Unknown |buried= |burial goods={{ill|Haji pottery|ja|土師器}} |cultural_property=Fukushima Prefecture Designated Historic Site: "Kinegamori Kofun" |notes=Possibly the oldest kofun in the Tohoku region }} '''Kinegamori Kofun''' ({{ill|Kinegamori Kofun|ja|杵ガ森古墳}}) is a {{ill|Keyhole-shaped Kofun|ja|前方後円墳|1=Zenpokoenfun}} located in {{ill|Aizubange|ja|会津坂下町}}, {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|ja|福島県}}. It is part of the {{ill|Unai Aotsu Kofun Group|ja|宇内青津古墳群}} and is designated as a Fukushima Prefecture Historic Site. Kinegamori Kofun is recognized as one of the oldest kofun in the Tohoku region. == Overview == Located west of the city center of Aizubange, along the western bank of the former Miyagawa River (Tsurunumagawa), this keyhole-shaped Kofun faces south. Surrounding it are other ancient Kofuns, including Usagamori Kofun and Maigamori Kofun (now disappeared), which illustrate the region's prosperity during the early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}}. The rear section of Kinegamori Kofun is a two-tiered construction, while the details of the front section are unclear due to erosion. Its distinctive paddle-shaped design resembles that of the earliest keyhole-shaped mound, {{ill|Hashihaka Kofun|ja|箸墓古墳}}, in {{ill|Nara Prefecture|ja|奈良県}}, though Kinegamori is about one-sixth the size of Hashihaka. The mound is encircled by a moat. The mound was first noted in the historical text ''Shinpen Aizu Fudoki'' as a mound, but it was officially identified during an archaeological survey in 1961. Before excavations, only part of the rear section was visible, with the remainder buried under rice fields. Excavations during 1990 revealed that the mound, previously thought to be circular, was actually a keyhole-shaped kofun. Additional findings included a surrounding moat, pit dwellings, and 10 {{ill|Square Kofuns|ja|方形周溝墓}}. Artifacts such as {{ill|Haji pottery|ja|土師器}} suggest the mound was constructed in the late 3rd to early 4th century, making it one of the oldest kofun in Tohoku. == Dimensions == The dimensions of the mound are as follows: * Total length: 45.6 meters * Rear section: ** Diameter: 25.6 meters ** Height: ~2 meters * Front section: ** Length: 24 meters ** Width: 19 meters == Inarizuka Site == [[File:Inarizuka-iseki (Aizubange).JPG|thumb|200px|right|Inarizuka Site]] The '''Inarizuka Site''' ({{ill|Inarizuka Site|ja|稲荷塚遺跡}}) is an early {{ill|Kofun period|ja|古墳時代}} site near Kinegamori Kofun. Excavations from 1990 to 1991 uncovered 15 pit dwellings, 3 {{ill|pillar-supported buildings|ja|掘立柱建物}}, and 10 square Kofuns. These findings suggest that the site initially functioned as a settlement during the early Kofun period. Kinegamori Kofun was later constructed on the site, with addition[[Category:addition]]al Kofuns built nearby for individuals associated with the kofun. In 2017, an addition[[Category:addition]]al 13 square Kofuns were discovered, bringing the total to 23, making it the largest collection of square Kofuns in Fukushima Prefecture[http://www.minyu-net.com/news/news/FM2017dd22-222147.php "Fukushima Prefecture’s Largest 'Square Kofun Group' Discovered at Inarizuka Site"] (''Minyu'', November 22, 2017).. == Cultural Property == === Fukushima Prefecture Designated Cultural Properties === * Historic Site ** Kinegamori Kofun - Designated on March 22, 1996[http://www.town.aizubange.fukushima.jp/soshiki/30/285.html "List of Cultural Properties in Aizubange"] (Aizubange Town website).. == References == {{Reflist}} == Bibliography == {{small|References used in this article}} * Historic Site Description Panel * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1993|chapter=Kinegamori Kofun|title={{ill|Historical Place names of Japan|ja|日本歴史地名大系}} 7: Fukushima Prefecture|publisher={{ill|Heibonsha|ja|平凡社}}|isbn=4582490077|ref={{Harvid|Kinegamori Kofun (Heibonsha)|1993}}}} * Hidehito Tsuji, *The Foundation of Tohoku Kofun Research: Aizu Otsukayama Kofun*, "Learning from Historical Sites" Series 029, Shinseisha, 2006. == Further Reading == {{small|Sources not directly used in the article}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1991|chapter=|title=Excavation Report on Kinegamori Kofun and Inarizuka Site (Aizubange Town Cultural Property Report No. 22)|publisher=Aizubange Town Board of Education|isbn=|ref=}} * {{Cite book|editor=|author=|year=1995|chapter=|title=Preservation Report of Kinegamori Kofun (Aizubange Town Cultural Property Report No. 33)|publisher=Aizubange Town Board of Education|isbn=|ref=}} == External Links == {{Commonscat|Kinegamori-kofun}} * [http://www.town.aizubange.fukushima.jp/soshiki/30/282.html Kinegamori Kofun] - Aizubange Town website {{Archaeology}} [[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:Kofun in Fukushima Prefecture]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:History of Aizubange]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:Zenpokoenfun]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:Fukushima Prefecture Designated Historic Sites]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:dd deprecated open]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:01-17]] [[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:Fixed Kofun templates]] [[Category:dd]] [[Category:dd]] [[ja:杵ガ森古墳]] [[Category:dd]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[Category:dd]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[Category:dd]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[Category:dd]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[Category:dd]] [[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[Category:Completion tier 6]][[Category: tier 6]][[Category:dd]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[Category:dd]] [[Category:dd]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[Category:dd]] {{DEFAULTSORT:Kinegamori Kofun}} [[Category:New]] [[Category:qq]][[sn:Kinegamori Kofun]][[Category:qq]] [[Category:New]] {{Kofun navbar}} [[Category:Marked infobox kofun with unknown parameters]]
